readings.title: System Dynamics Modeling for Evaluating Regional Hydrologic and Economic Effects of Irrigation Efficiency Policy
readings.notes: format: None; original topic/subtopic: 'Supplementary topics' / 'Watershed modeling'
readings.abstract: Exploring the dynamic mechanisms of coupled sociohydrologic systems is necessary to solve future water sustainability issues. This paper employs system dynamics modeling to determine hydrologic and economic implications of an irrigation efficiency (IE) policy (increased conveyance efficiency and field efficiency) in a coupled sociohydrologic system with three climate scenarios. Simulations are conducted within the lower Rio Grande region (LRG) of New Mexico for the years 1969 to 2099, including water, land, capital, and population modules. Quadrant analysis is utilized to compare the IE policy outcomes with the base case and to categorize results of simulations according to hydrologic and economic sustainability. The four categories are beneficial, unacceptable, unsustainable agricultural development, and unsustainable hydrology. Simulation results for the IE policy analyzed here fall into the categories of unsustainable agricultural development or unacceptable, suggesting there are long-term negative effects to regional economies in all scenarios with mixed results for hydrologic variables. IE policy can yield water for redistribution as increased unit water supply in the field produces more deep percolation; however, IE policy sacrifices regional connectivity. Specifically, simulation results show that the policy increases abundance by 4.7–74.5% and return flow by −3.0–9.9%. These positive results, however, come at the cost of decreased hydrologic connectivity (−31.5 to −25.1%) and negative economic impacts (−32.7 to −5.7%). Long-term net depletions in groundwater are also observed from loss of hydrologic connectivity and increased agricultural water demand from projections of increased consumptive use of crops. Adaptive water management that limits water use in drought years and replenishes groundwater in abundant years as well as economic incentives to offset the costs of infrastructure improvements will be necessary for the IE policy to result in sustainable agriculture and water resources.

readings.title: A practical guide to MaxEnt for modeling species’ distributions: what it does, and why inputs and settings matter
readings.notes: format: Tutorial; original topic/subtopic: 'Supplementary topics' / 'Species distribution modeling'
readings.abstract: The MaxEnt software package is one of the most popular tools for species distribution and environmental niche modeling, with over 1000 published applications since 2006. Its popularity is likely for two reasons: 1) MaxEnt typically outperforms other methods based on predictive accuracy and 2) the software is particularly easy to use. MaxEnt users must make a number of decisions about how they should select their input data and choose from a wide variety of settings in the software package to build models from these data. The underlying basis for making these decisions is unclear in many studies, and default settings are apparently chosen, even though alternative settings are often more appropriate. In this paper, we provide a detailed explanation of how MaxEnt works and a prospectus on modeling options to enable users to make informed decisions when preparing data, choosing settings and interpreting output. We explain how the choice of background samples reflects prior assumptions, how nonlinear functions of environmental variables (features) are created and selected, how to account for environmentally biased sampling, the interpretation of the various types of model output and the challenges for model evaluation. We demonstrate MaxEnt's calculations using both simplified simulated data and occurrence data from South Africa on species of the flowering plant family Proteaceae. Throughout, we show how MaxEnt's outputs vary in response to different settings to highlight the need for making biologically motivated modeling decisions.

readings.title: Predicting wasteful spending in tree planting programs in Indian Himalaya
readings.notes: format: None; original topic/subtopic: 'Supplementary topics' / 'Species distribution modeling'

readings.title: Monitoring biodiversity in the Anthropocene using remote sensing in species distribution models
readings.notes: format: None; original topic/subtopic: 'Supplementary topics' / 'Species distribution modeling'
readings.abstract: In the face of the growing challenges brought about by human activities, effective planning and decision-making in biodiversity and ecosystem conservation, restoration, and sustainable development are urgently needed. Ecological models can play a key role in supporting this need and helping to safeguard the natural assets that underpin human wellbeing and support life on land and below water (United Nations Sustainable Development Goals; SDG 15 & 14). The urgency and complexity of safeguarding forest (SDG 15.2) and mountain ecosystems (SDG 15.4), for example, and halting decline in biodiversity (SDG 15.5) in the Anthropocene requires a re-envisioning of how ecological models can best support the comprehensive assessments of biodiversity and its change that are required for successful action. A key opportunity to advance ecological modeling for both predictive and explanatory purposes arises through a collaboration between ecologists and the Earth observation community, and a close integration of remote sensing and species distribution models. Remote sensing products have the capacity to provide continuous spatiotemporal information about key factors driving the distribution of organisms, therefore improving both the use and accuracy of these models for management and planning. Here we first survey the literature on remote sensing data products available to ecological modelers interested in improving predictions of species range dynamics under global change. We specifically explore the key biophysical processes underlying the distribution of species in the Anthropocene including climate variability, changes in land cover, and disturbances. We then discuss potential synergies between the ecological modeling and remote sensing communities, and highlight opportunities to close the data and conceptual gaps that currently impede a more effective application of remote sensing for the monitoring and modeling of ecological systems. Specific attention is given to how potential collaborations between the two communities could lead to new opportunities to report on progress towards global agendas - such as the Agenda 2030 for sustainable development of the United Nations or the Post-2020 Global Biodiversity Framework of the Convention for Biological Diversity, and help guide conservation and management strategies towards sustainability.

readings.title: Climate risks to soy-maize double-cropping due to Amazon deforestation
readings.notes: format: Tutorial; original topic/subtopic: 'Supplementary topics' / 'Agriculture - Forests - Climate linkages'
readings.abstract: AbstractDeforestation in the Brazilian Amazon (BA) for cattle and soybean production has significant consequences for the various aspects of the climate system. Land surface modifications due to deforestation directly influence surface energy and moisture availability, hence impacting rainfall patterns, air temperature and the onset of the agricultural rainy season. Here, we assess the forest loss‐related climate risks for the first and second crop seasons of the soy‐maize double cropping system in the BA. We utilized long‐term, daily, remote sensed climate data and annual land‐use maps as input for a machine learning algorithm to isolate the signal of forest loss on the climate. Our findings indicate that forest loss in the BA intensifies the risks of climate change from the local to the regional geographical scale, with the impact being more pronounced at the regional scale. Between 1999 and 2019, largely deforested regions exhibited a delay of approximately 76 days in the onset of the agricultural rainy season. These regions also experienced a 360 mm decrease in rainfall and an increase in maximum air temperature of 2.5°C. In view of these results, there are collective advantages of halting deforestation. Conservation of the Amazon Forest is vital for maintaining the early onset of the agricultural rainy season, favourable temperatures and adequate rainfall volume needed for attaining high yields in the soy‐maize double cropping system.

readings.title: Warming inhibits increases in vegetation net primary productivity despite greening in India
readings.notes: format: None; original topic/subtopic: 'Supplementary topics' / 'Agriculture - Forests - Climate linkages'
readings.abstract: AbstractIndia is the second-highest contributor to the post-2000 global greening. However, with satellite data, here we show that this 18.51% increase in Leaf Area Index (LAI) during 2001–2019 fails to translate into increased carbon uptake due to warming constraints. Our analysis further shows 6.19% decrease in Net Primary Productivity (NPP) during 2001–2019 over the temporally consistent forests in India despite 6.75% increase in LAI. We identify hotspots of statistically significant decreasing trends in NPP over the key forested regions of Northeast India, Peninsular India, and the Western Ghats. Together, these areas contribute to more than 31% of the NPP of India (1274.8 TgC.year−1). These three regions are also the warming hotspots in India. Granger Causality analysis confirms that temperature causes the changes in net-photosynthesis of vegetation. Decreasing photosynthesis and stable respiration, above a threshold temperature, over these regions, as seen in observations, are the key reasons behind the declining NPP. Our analysis shows that warming has already started affecting carbon uptake in Indian forests and calls for improved climate resilient forest management practices in a warming world.

readings.title: Can remote sensing enable a Biomass Climate Adaptation Index for agricultural systems?
readings.notes: format: None; original topic/subtopic: 'Supplementary topics' / 'Agriculture - Forests - Climate linkages'
readings.abstract: Systematic tools and approaches for measuring climate change adaptation at multiple scales of spatial resolution are lacking, limiting measurement of progress toward the adaptation goals of the Paris Agreement. In particular, there is a lack of adaptation measurement or tracking systems that are coherent (measuring adaptation itself), comparable (allowing comparisons across geographies and systems), and comprehensive (are supported by the necessary data). In addition, most adaptation measurement efforts lack an appropriate counterfactual baseline to assess the effectiveness of adaptation-related interventions. To address this, we are developing a “Biomass Climate Adaptation Index” (Biomass CAI) for agricultural systems, where climate adaptation progress across multiple scales can be measured by satellite remote sensing. The Biomass CAI can be used at global, national, landscape and farm-level to remotely monitor agri-biomass productivity associated with adaptation interventions, and to facilitate more tailored “precision adaptation”. The Biomass CAI places focus on decision-support for end-users to ensure that the most effective climate change adaptation investments and interventions can be made in agricultural and food systems.

readings.title: Everybody Loves a Good Drought: Stories from India's Poorest Districts
readings.authors: Sainath, P.
readings.notes: This is a book. Classic reportage on rural poverty, drought, and agrarian distress across India's poorest districts; grounding/context reading for the agriculture problem_discovery classes.
readings.abstract: A compilation of roughly 80 essays written for the Times of India between 1990 and 1992, based on reporting from ten of India's poorest districts across Orissa, Tamil Nadu, Madhya Pradesh, and Bihar. Documents the lived reality behind poverty statistics: the inefficiency of relief and poverty-alleviation programmes, funds failing to reach the poorest, displacement from 'development' projects, forest and agricultural land destruction, debt cycles from exploitative moneylending, and the disproportionate impact on tribal (Adivasi) and lower-caste (Dalit) communities. Won the Ramon Magsaysay Award and remains a widely cited classic of rural-poverty reportage in India, prescribed in over 100 universities.

readings.title: Ramrao: The Story of India's Farm Crisis
readings.authors: Hardikar, Jaideep
readings.notes: This is a book. Biographical account of one farmer's life used to unpack the structural causes of India's contemporary agrarian/farm crisis (debt, price risk, climate stress).
readings.abstract: One morning in 2014, Ramrao Panchleniwar, an ordinary cotton grower in Maharashtra's Vidarbha region, consumed two bottles of pesticide in a suicide attempt but miraculously survived. Rural journalist Jaideep Hardikar tracked Ramrao's life over the following seven years, using it as a window into India's decades-old farm crisis: the collapse of small-farm cotton cultivation, the debt spiral behind India's epidemic of farmer suicides, and the everyday struggles, failings, and resilience of an Indian farmer's life. The result of years of committed reportage, the book turns one ordinary life into what reviewers have called an essential biography for our times.

readings.title: Southern Insurgency: The Coming of the Global Working Class
readings.authors: Ness, Immanuel
readings.notes: This is a book. Global-South-centered account of the changing composition and organizing power of the working class, shifting the labour narrative away from a Global-North-centric framing.

readings.title: The Broken Ladder: The Paradox and Potential of India's One-Billion
readings.authors: Krishna, Anirudh
readings.notes: This is a book. Mapped to health per instructor's framing; substantively about the structural barriers (including health shocks) that keep capable individuals from escaping poverty despite growth -- a bridge between health, livelihood, and social-capital themes.
readings.abstract: Despite India's rapid economic growth, why do opportunities remain so unevenly available, with a 'dollar economy' and a 'rupee economy' persisting side by side? Drawing on decades of fieldwork living in villages and urban slum communities, Krishna documents the everyday lives of individuals whose talent and effort go unrewarded due to structural barriers -- health shocks, weak information and networks, and thin local opportunity -- rather than lack of ability. Covers preventing future poverty, the deep but untapped pool of talent among the poor, and democratic participation 'at the doorstep', arguing that growing the economy and developing individuals' capabilities must go together for growth to translate into broadly shared opportunity.
readings.venue: Cambridge University Press / Penguin Random House India

readings.title: Marginlands: Indian Landscapes on the Brink
readings.authors: Kumar-Rao, Arati
readings.notes: This is a book. Narrative nonfiction/photojournalism across India's threatened landscapes (Thar desert water systems, Gangetic dolphin habitat, Sundarban mangroves, Kerala coastline); grounding reading for water_security problem_discovery with strong climate_change relevance.
readings.abstract: A work of environmental nature writing and photojournalism traversing India's most threatened landscapes: sand-dune mining in the Thar desert that undermines centuries-old water-harvesting systems, the fragmented habitat of the endangered Gangetic dolphin, human-wildlife tension in the Sundarban mangroves, coastal flooding from Mumbai encroachment, and erosion threatening fishing communities along the Kerala coastline. Combining first-hand reportage with art and photography, documents the misguided decisions, ignored warnings, and climate-change-compounded disasters pushing these landscapes toward collapse, while also surfacing traditional ecological knowledge that communities have used to survive in them for centuries.

readings.title: What's Left of the Jungle: A Conservation Story
readings.authors: Sekar, Nitin
readings.notes: This is a book. Ethnographic-style narrative from Buxa Tiger Reserve on human-elephant conflict and the lived tradeoffs of wildlife conservation for nearby rural communities; grounding reading for wildlife_conservation problem_discovery.
readings.abstract: A dual narrative alternating between the author's years as a Princeton PhD student conducting field research on Asian elephants at Buxa Tiger Reserve, West Bengal, and the life story of Akshu Atri, his field assistant, born and raised on the reserve's fringes. Indian officials estimate over half a million families lose crops or property to wild elephants each year; Akshu's family has lost crops, property, and neighbours to elephants, yet neither hates them. Through Akshu's story of childhood destitution, entanglements with poachers and smugglers, and his path out of poverty, the book unravels the complex affection rural communities hold for jungle wildlife despite its real costs, and what sustaining that coexistence might require.

readings.title: Headlines From the Heartland: Reinventing the Hindi Public Sphere
readings.authors: Ninan, Sevanti
readings.notes: This is a book. Account of the explosive growth of the Hindi-language press and its remaking of the rural/small-town public sphere in India; grounding reading for media_information problem_discovery.
readings.abstract: Based on over 150 interviews with journalists, readers, publishers, politicians, administrators, and activists, plus original content analysis and field study across eight states, traces the 1990s-onward rural newspaper revolution in Hindi-speaking north and central India. As literacy, communications, and purchasing power rose in small towns and villages, Hindi newspapers surged in readership while localising furiously. Describes the emergence and reinvention of a local public sphere by new non-elite readership, effects on politics and administration, the shift from editor-led to reader-led and marketing-driven newspapers, the rise of village-level citizen journalists, and the impact of caste and communalism on the Hindi press.

readings.title: Active Social Capital: Tracing the Roots of Development and Democracy
readings.authors: Krishna, Anirudh
readings.notes: This is a book. General theory distinguishing 'active' social capital (agency-building) from stock social capital, and its role in development and democratic outcomes; area-agnostic since the theory applies across sectors, filed under the social_capital_marginalization axis rather than any one area.
readings.abstract: The idea of social capital allows scholars to assess the quality of relationships among people within a community and show how that quality affects the ability to achieve shared goals. With evidence collected from sixty-nine villages in India, Krishna investigates what social capital is, how it operates in practice, and what results it can be expected to produce. Distinguishes a structuralist hypothesis (social capital is merely a byproduct of structures) from an agency hypothesis (social capital matters, but agency -- provided by new, non-ascriptive village political entrepreneurs -- is needed to convert its stock into a flow of benefits), and tests these against economic development outcomes, community harmony/peace, and democratic participation across villages in Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh.

readings.title: Technology and (Dis)Empowerment: A Call to Technologists
readings.authors: Seth, Aaditeshwar
readings.notes: This is a book. Instructor's own book (Emerald Publishing, foreword by Tim Unwin), arguing the primary goal of technology should be to overturn unjust societal structures rather than assume neutral empowerment; spans problem framing, design ethos, deployment-scale critique (digital divide, algorithmic bias, misplaced e-governance assumptions), and organizational/operational theory (collectivizing technologists' own governance) -- genuinely area-agnostic across the whole course.
readings.abstract: Draws attention to the varying factors by which technology, including that built with explicit 'social good' intentions, often produces disempowerment effects instead. Argues social good must be defined rigorously as a consequentialist commitment to terminal values -- centrally, power-based equality -- rather than instrumental guardrails like privacy or 'do no harm' alone, and that achieving it requires technologists to move beyond one-time ethical design into ongoing socio-technical management of deployed systems, to organize collectively against the structural forces keeping them apolitical, and to help build the societal communication infrastructure needed for democratic, pluralistic deliberation about values. Draws on theorists including Marx, Sen, Habermas, Gramsci, Foucault, Illich, and Escobar, alongside case studies including Aadhaar, Facebook, and the author's own Mobile Vaani platform.
readings.venue: Emerald Publishing Limited (foreword by Tim Unwin, UNESCO Chair in ICT4D)

readings.title: SHIELD: A Self-supervised, Silicosis-focused Hierarchical Imaging Framework for Occupational Lung Disease Diagnosis
readings.authors: Akhter, Yasmeena; Ranjan, Rishabh; Singh, Richa; Vatsa, Mayank
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Occupational lung disease (silicosis) diagnosis from chest X-rays, validated on a 3044-sample dataset from public health centers in India; directly relevant to industrial/informal labour health risk.
readings.abstract: Silicosis is an irreversible lung disease caused by silica dust exposure in industrial settings. Early detection is crucial, but automatic diagnostic methods are hindered by limited data availability. We propose SHIELD - a self-supervised, Silicosis-focused Hierarchical Imaging framework for early occupational Lung disease Diagnosis. Our method leverages a multi-resolution jigsaw puzzle pretext task on CXR images to extract and preserve features for lung region analysis. By employing a pyramidal strategy to generate pretrained models at various resolutions, followed by fine-tuning and a two-level ensembling across diverse deep learning architectures, SHIELD achieves enhanced diagnostic accuracy. We validate our approach on a publicly collected CXR dataset of 3044 samples from public health centers in India. SHIELD achieves 72% accuracy, demonstrating up to 20% improvement over baseline approaches. This work advances medical image analysis and supports UN Sustainable Development Goal 3 by providing cost-effective early screening in resource-limited settings.

readings.title: SAHAY: Multimodal, Privacy-Preserving AI for Suicide Risk Detection and Intervention in India
readings.authors: Singh, Salam Michael; Sethi, Manik Inder Singh; Math, Suresh Bada; Chakraborty, Tanmoy
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: SENSITIVE TOPIC (suicide risk prediction/intervention) -- included as an academic reference on mental-health AI system design and India's TeleMANAS integration; instructors should preview before assigning given the subject matter and consider content-warning framing in class.
readings.abstract: Suicide accounts for one of the leading causes of death in India, with over 164,033 deaths reported in 2021. Despite increased awareness, the gap between the need for consistent treatment and actual accessibility remains a challenge due to limited mental health infrastructure, the stigma surrounding mental illness in society, and the lack of real-time detection mechanisms. In this proposal, we strive to design SAHAY, an AI-based suicide prevention framework that couples prediction with prevention and treatment access. SAHAY is culturally adaptive, multilingual and integrates with India's TeleMANAS mental health support system, aligning with UN SDGs 3, 4, 5, 10, and 17.

readings.title: Leveraging Artificial Intelligence to Bridge Gaps in Pediatric Oncology Care for Marginalized Spanish-Speaking Communities
readings.authors: Khvatskii, Grigorii; Garcia Martinez, Angelica; Pan, Deng; Belcher, Matthew; Medrano Loera, Geronimo; Pineda Perez, Dayana; Ferrari Munoz-Ledo, Juan Emmanuel; Marquez-Gonzalez, Horacio; Moniz, Nuno; Chawla, Nitesh V.
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: LLM+ASR system to bridge communication gaps between hospital staff and caregivers in low- and middle-income country healthcare systems; explicit LMIC framing and deployment-with-existing-infrastructure design ethos.
readings.abstract: In low-and middle-income countries (LMICs) pediatric cancer patients and their caregivers often suffer from effects of underfunded, fragmented and outdated healthcare systems. One of these effects is a breakdown of communication between hospital staff and caregivers, which is felt stronger among vulnerable populations. Our proposed solution integrates Large Language Models (LLM) and Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) technologies to enhance communication between caregivers and healthcare providers while integrating community feedback. We combine cutting-edge technology with existing hospital infrastructure to allow for easy deployment and testing. The system will improve access to health, nutrition, and parental care programs, prioritizing caregiver engagement and real-time interaction.

readings.title: DECASTE: Unveiling Caste Stereotypes in Large Language Models Through Multi-Dimensional Bias Analysis
readings.authors: Vijayaraghavan, Prashanth; Vosoughi, Soroush; Chiazor, Lamogha; Horesh, Raya; Abreu de Paula, Rogerio; Degan, Ehsan; Mukherjee, Vandana
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Directly targets the caste axis, which was previously the thinnest cross-cutting axis in the reading list; evaluates caste bias in LLMs across socio-cultural, economic, educational, and political dimensions using India-specific Dalit/Shudra vs. dominant-caste framing.
readings.abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing (NLP) and expanded their applications across diverse domains. However, despite their impressive capabilities, LLMs have been shown to reflect and perpetuate harmful societal biases. A critical and underexplored issue is the reinforcement of caste-based biases, particularly towards India's marginalized caste groups such as Dalits and Shudras. In this paper, we address this gap by proposing DECASTE, a novel, multi-dimensional framework designed to detect and assess both implicit and explicit caste biases in LLMs across four dimensions: socio-cultural, economic, educational, and political. By benchmarking several state-of-the-art LLMs, we reveal that these models systematically reinforce caste biases, with significant disparities observed in the treatment of oppressed versus dominant caste groups.

readings.title: Detection and Geographic Localization of Natural Objects in the Wild: A Case Study on Palms
readings.authors: Cui, Kangning; Zhu, Rongkun; Wang, Manqi; Tang, Wei; Larsen, Gregory D.; Pauca, Victor P.; Alqahtani, Sarra; Yang, Fan; Segurado, David; Lutz, David A.; Morel, Jean-Michel; Silman, Miles R.
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: UAV-orthomosaic-based palm detection pipeline (PRISM) across 21 ecologically diverse sites in western Ecuador; palms are used as an indicator of tropical forest health and local economic dependence.
readings.abstract: Palms are ecologically and economically indicators of tropical forest health, biodiversity, and human impact that support local economies and global forest product supply chains. While palm detection in plantations is well-studied, efforts to map naturally occurring palms in dense forests remain limited. We develop PRISM (Processing, Inference, Segmentation, and Mapping), a flexible pipeline for detecting and localizing palms in dense tropical forests using large orthomosaic images, constructing a UAV-derived dataset across 21 ecologically diverse sites in western Ecuador, annotated with 8,830 bounding boxes and 5,026 palm center points.

readings.title: AI-Assisted Triage and Decision Support of Head and Neck Cancer Screening and Diagnosis in Low-Resourced Settings
readings.authors: Lee, Min Hun; Lam, Sean Shao Wei; Liew, Shaun Xin Hong; Dorosan, Michael; Graves, Nicholas; Karlstrom, Jonas; Tan, Hiang Khoon; Lee, Walter Tsong
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Multi-site (Singapore, US, Bangladesh) collaborative AI system for head-and-neck cancer triage; explicitly engages clinician trust/adoption barriers to AI in resource-limited settings, not just the technical method.
readings.abstract: The mortality burden of head and neck cancer (HNC) is increasing globally and disproportionately affects people in low-and middle-income countries with limited medical workforce. AI algorithms are increasingly explored to process medical imaging data, but clinical adoption remains challenging as clinicians struggle to trust and understand AI. Our international and interdisciplinary team collaborates with clinicians from multiple sites (Singapore, the U.S., and Bangladesh) to collect a diverse, multi-site dataset and develop computational techniques -- a YOLOv5-based glottis detector, a classifier of patient status, uncertainty quantification, and interactive Vision Language Model-based AI explanations -- to improve collaboration between clinicians and AI for triage and diagnosis, particularly in resource-limited settings.

readings.title: Classifying and Tracking International Aid Contribution Towards SDGs
readings.authors: Park, Sungwon; Lee, Dongjoon; Ahn, Kyeongjin; Choi, Yubin; Lee, Junho; Cha, Meeyoung; Park, Kyung Ryul
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: AI model developed with government agency partners to classify and track international aid contributions against SDG categories over multiple years; relevant to governance/aid-effectiveness monitoring.
readings.abstract: International aid is a critical mechanism for promoting economic growth and well-being in developing nations, supporting progress toward the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). However, tracking aid contributions remains challenging due to labor-intensive data management, incomplete records, and the heterogeneous nature of aid data. We partnered with government agencies to develop an AI model that complements manual classification and mitigates human bias in subjective interpretation, integrating SDG-specific semantics and prior knowledge from language models. Applied to a comprehensive multi-year dataset, our model reveals hidden trends in the temporal evolution of international development cooperation, empowering policymakers with data-driven decision-making tools.

readings.title: MCloudNet: An Ultra-Short-Term Photovoltaic Power Forecasting Framework With Multi-Layer Cloud Coverage
readings.authors: Wan, Meng; Liu, Tiantian; Bi, Yuxuan; Wang, Jue; Cui, Hui; Cao, Rongqiang; Wang, Jiaxiang; Shi, Peng; Nie, Ningming; Wang, Yangang
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Deployed system supporting rural photovoltaic poverty-alleviation power stations across ~60,000 Chinese villages; strong parallel to the course's electricity-access/infrastructure readings, from a different developing-region context (rural China rather than India).
readings.abstract: Over 4.15 million low-income households across nearly 60,000 villages in China benefit from photovoltaic (PV) poverty alleviation power stations. However, weak infrastructure and limited capabilities make these systems vulnerable to fluctuations. This paper proposes MCloudNet, a multi-modal framework designed to improve ultra-short-term PV prediction in data-scarce, cloud-dynamic environments, modeling multi-layer cloud structures from satellite imagery fused with time-series meteorological data. A province-level dispatch system with MCloudNet has been deployed in Hebei, reducing over 60 million kWh of solar curtailment and benefiting approximately 50,000 rural households.

readings.title: Faster Annotation for Elevation-Guided Flood Extent Mapping by Consistency-Enhanced Active Learning
readings.authors: Adhikari, Saugat; Yan, Da; Wang, Tianyang; Dyken, Landon; Kumar, Sidharth; Yuan, Lyuheng; Ahmad, Akhlaque; Han, Jiao; Zhou, Yang; Petruzza, Steve
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: General active-learning method to cut flood-map annotation time from hours to ~20 minutes; not tied to a developing-region study site, but the technique (minimizing costly expert annotation) is directly relevant to low-resource flood mapping.
readings.abstract: Flood extent mapping is crucial for disaster response and damage assessment. While Earth imagery and terrain data (in the form of DEM) are now readily available, there are few flood annotation data for training machine learning models, which hinders the automated mapping of flooded areas. We propose ALFA, an interactive active-learning-based approach to minimize the annotators' efforts when preparing the ground-truth flood map in a satellite image. ALFA calibrates the prediction consistency of a segmentation model across training cycles and for various data augmentations, and recommends the superpixels the model is most uncertain about. Extensive experiments on various regions hit by flooding show we can improve annotation time from hours to around 20 minutes.

readings.title: IGraSS: Learning to Identify Infrastructure Networks from Satellite Imagery by Iterative Graph-constrained Semantic Segmentation
readings.authors: Hoque, Oishee Bintey; Adiga, Abhijin; Adiga, Aniruddha; Chaudhary, Siddharth; Marathe, Madhav V.; Ravi, S.S.; Rajagopalan, Kirti; Wilson, Amanda; Swarup, Samarth
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Graph-constrained segmentation method for mapping canal (irrigation) and road networks from satellite imagery when ground truth is incomplete -- directly reusable methodology for irrigation infrastructure mapping in data-poor settings.
readings.abstract: Accurate canal network mapping is essential for water management, including irrigation planning and infrastructure maintenance. State-of-the-art semantic segmentation models rely on large, well-annotated remote sensing datasets; incomplete or inadequate ground truth can hinder these approaches. This paper develops IGraSS, an iterative framework combining a semantic segmentation module with a graph-based ground-truth refinement module that views the infrastructure network as a graph. Experiments show IGraSS reduces unreachable canal segments from ~18% to ~3%, and demonstrates generalizability using road networks as an example.

readings.title: Knowledge-Informed Deep Learning for Irrigation Type Mapping from Remote Sensing
readings.authors: Hoque, Oishee Bintey; Mandal, Nibir Chandra; Adiga, Abhijin; Swarup, Samarth; Nouwakpo, Sayjro Kossi; Wilson, Amanda; Marathe, Madhav
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Study sites are five US states, not a developing region, but the method (transfer learning with only 40% of training data reaching baseline performance) is exactly the kind of low-label-budget technique that matters most in data-scarce developing-region settings -- included under the relaxed cs_fundamentals rule.
readings.abstract: Accurate mapping of irrigation methods is crucial for sustainable agricultural practices and food systems. Existing models relying solely on spectral features are ineffective due to the complexity of agricultural landscapes and limited training data. We present Knowledge-Informed Irrigation Mapping (KIIM), a Swin-Transformer based approach using a specialized projection matrix, spatial attention, bi-directional cross-attention, and a weighted ensemble. Experiments on five US states show up to 22.9% IoU improvement over baseline, and a two-phase transfer learning approach achieves a 51% IoU boost in a state with limited labeled data -- reaching baseline performance with only 40% of the training data.

readings.title: Expanding Connected Components from Alternative Terminals: Global Optimization for Freshwater Fishes Under the UN's 30x30 Conservation Goal
readings.authors: Mao, Yue; Qu, Zhongdi; Miqueleiz, Imanol; Ferber, Aaron; Wolf, Sami; Grimson, Marc; Heilpern, Sebastian; Pacheco, Felipe S.; Flecker, Alexander S.; McIntyre, Peter B.; Gomes, Carla P.
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Global-scope graph optimization (~200,000 freshwater basins, ~13,000 species) for protected-area selection; a general optimization methodology for conservation planning that generalizes to any country's freshwater systems, including India's.
readings.abstract: Climate change and biodiversity loss are among humanity's most pressing challenges. Under the UN's Target 3 ('30x30'), countries seek to protect 30% of terrestrial, inland water, coastal, and marine areas by 2030. We address the UN 30x30 target for global freshwater fish conservation by introducing the Expansion of Connected Components from Alternative Terminals Problem, a graph-based optimization problem capturing ecological priorities and connectivity constraints. We analyze its computational complexity, propose novel integer programming formulations, and demonstrate scalability to a global real-world scope encompassing approximately 200,000 freshwater basins and 13,000 species.

readings.title: Towards the 30 by 30 Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework Target: Optimising Graph Connectivity in Constraint-Based Spatial Planning
readings.authors: Le Bozec-Chiffoleau, Sulian; Justeau-Allaire, Dimitri; Lorca, Xavier; Prud'homme, Charles; Simonin, Gilles; Vismara, Philippe; Birnbaum, Philippe; Rinck, Nicolas; Beldiceanu, Nicolas
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Constraint-programming method for budget-constrained forest-connectivity restoration planning, case-studied in New Caledonia; the CP formulation and Hanan-grid preprocessing generalize to restoration planning in any resource-constrained forest system.
readings.abstract: The Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework aims to protect 30% of terrestrial, inland water, marine, and coastal ecosystems worldwide, with at least 30% under effective restoration by 2030, requiring ecological connectivity between natural habitats. Most spatial planning models treat connectivity as a constraint via Steiner trees, which is inappropriate when managers seek to optimise connectivity under a budget constraint. We formalise this as the budget-constrained graph connectivity optimisation problem, illustrate why naive constraint-programming approaches are inefficient using a real case study (forest connectivity restoration in New Caledonia), and provide a Hanan-grid-based preprocessing method that preserves an optimal solution.

readings.title: DeepShade: Enable Shade Simulation by Text-conditioned Image Generation
readings.authors: Da, Longchao; Liu, Xiangrui; Shivakoti, Mithun; Kutralingam, Thirulogasankar Pranav; Yang, Yezhou; Wei, Hua
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Diffusion-based shade estimation to support heatwave-aware routing/urban planning, demonstrated in Tempe, Arizona; the underlying technique (estimating shade from noisy satellite imagery) is directly transferable to heat-stressed urban areas in developing regions with far less ground-truth data available.
readings.abstract: Heatwaves pose a significant threat to public health, especially as global warming intensifies. Current routing systems fail to incorporate shade information due to the difficulty of estimating shade directly from noisy satellite imagery and limited training data for generative models. We build an extensive dataset of simulated building shadows aligned with satellite images across diverse regions, and propose DeepShade, a diffusion-based model that learns and synthesizes shade variations over time, conditioning on textual descriptions (time of day, solar angles). We demonstrate utility by calculating shade ratios for real-world route planning in Tempe, Arizona, and argue the approach benefits urban planning in extreme heat.

readings.title: Exploring Multimodal Foundation AI and Expert-in-the-Loop for Sustainable Management of Wild Salmon Fisheries in Indigenous Rivers
readings.authors: Xu, Chi; Jin, Yili; Ma, Sami; Qian, Rongsheng; Fang, Hao; Liu, Jiangchuan; Liu, Xue; Ngai, Edith C.H.; Atlas, William I.; Connors, Katrina M.; Spoljaric, Mark A.
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: Multimodal foundation-model + expert-in-the-loop monitoring for fisheries co-managed with Indigenous stewardship practitioners in remote, infrastructure-poor rivers; the participatory expert-in-the-loop design pattern (not just the technique) is relevant to community-governed natural-resource monitoring generally.
readings.abstract: Wild salmon are essential to the ecological, economic, and cultural sustainability of the North Pacific Rim. Yet climate variability, habitat loss, and data limitations in remote ecosystems lacking basic infrastructure pose significant challenges to effective fisheries management. This project explores integrating multimodal foundation AI and expert-in-the-loop frameworks to enhance wild salmon monitoring in Indigenous rivers across the Pacific Northwest, using video and sonar-based monitoring to develop AI-powered tools for automated species identification, counting, and length measurement, bringing together university researchers, fisheries biologists, Indigenous stewardship practitioners, government agencies, and conservation organizations.

readings.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Efficient and Fair Allocation of Healthcare Resources
readings.authors: Li, Yikuan; Mao, Chengsheng; Huang, Kaixuan; Wang, Hanyin; Yu, Zheng; Wang, Mengdi; Luo, Yuan
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: General RL-based method for fair rationing of scarce healthcare resources (framed around ventilators/pandemics); not set in a developing region, but resource scarcity and inequitable ad hoc allocation are exactly the conditions common in under-resourced health systems -- a technique worth exposing students to for adaptation.
readings.abstract: The scarcity of health care resources, such as ventilators, often leads to the unavoidable consequence of rationing, particularly during public health emergencies or in resource-constrained settings. The absence of a universally accepted standard for resource allocation protocols results in governments relying on varying criteria and heuristic-based approaches, often yielding suboptimal and inequitable outcomes. This study addresses fair and effective critical care resource allocation using deep reinforcement learning, proposing a transformer-based deep Q-network integrating individual patient disease progression and inter-patient interaction effects. Experiments show the approach significantly reduces excess deaths and achieves more equitable resource allocation compared to severity- and comorbidity-based protocols currently in use.

readings.title: Enhancing Online Climate Discourse: A Two-Stage Framework for Climate Content Categorization and Moderation
readings.authors: Upadhyaya, Apoorva; Nejdl, Wolfgang; Fisichella, Marco
readings.venue: IJCAI 2025 Special Track on AI and Social Good
readings.notes: NLP framework to categorize climate content (adaptation/resilience/denial) and moderate misinformation -- directly extends the course's existing media_information + climate_change readings (media bias, information diffusion) into the LLM-content-moderation era.
readings.abstract: Climate change requires both scientific solutions and effective communication, and online content plays a key role in shaping climate narratives. Prior research has mainly focused on public perception or categorized content by topic (impacts, mitigation, policy). We propose a novel multi-stage framework where stage 1 categorizes climate-related content into adaptation, resilience, and denial while stage 2 moderates content by enhancing or intervening based on alignment with climate goals, using a novel dataset of manually annotated tweets and news articles with a taxonomy developed by domain experts.

readings.title: India's rich and poor
readings.notes: Intro-video framing content for the 2022 course's contemporary-challenges block.

readings.title: Oxfam India CEO Behar: Inequality in India is 'absolutely obscene' | DW News
readings.notes: Intro-video framing content for the 2022 course's contemporary-challenges block.
readings.abstract:
Inequality is increasing around the world, but it's mostly coming at the cost of women and girls. That’s the message in a report on inequality from the charity Oxfam. The report highlights for instance the amount of unpaid work women and girls do around the world: 12.5 billion hours spent each day cooking, cleaning and looking after children and the elderly. More specifically in the case of India, earlier Oxfam research has shown that unpaid work by women looking after their homes and children is worth 3.1 percent of India’s GDP. While many women in India are unable to join the paid workforce, even for those who do, cultural factors remain a daily challenge. In Davos, DW's Janelle Dumalaon discussed the problem of inequality in India with Oxfam India CEO Amitabh Behar.

readings.title: To save the climate, we have to reimagine capitalism | Rebecca Henderson
readings.notes: Intro-video framing content for the 2022 course's contemporary-challenges block.
readings.abstract:
Take action on climate change at http://countdown.ted.com.

"Business is screwed if we don't fix climate change," says economist Rebecca Henderson. In this bold talk, she describes how unchecked capitalism destabilizes the environment and harms human health -- and makes the case for companies to step up and help fix the climate crisis they're causing. Hear what a reimagined capitalism, in which companies pay for the climate damage they cause, could look like.

This talk was part of the Countdown Global Launch on 10.10.2020. (Watch the full event here: https://youtu.be/5dVcn8NjbwY.) Countdown is TED's global initiative to accelerate solutions to the climate crisis. The goal: to build a better future by cutting greenhouse gas emissions in half by 2030, in the race to a zero-carbon world. Get involved at https://countdown.ted.com/sign-up

readings.notes: Intro-video framing content for the 2022 course's contemporary-challenges block; companion video to '2022_farmer_protests_2018a'.
readings.abstract:
#koraput #climatechange #rayagada 

In Rayagada, Bt cotton acreage has risen by 5,200 per cent in 16 years. The result: this biodiversity hotspot, rich in indigenous millets, rice varieties and forest foods, is seeing an alarming ecological shift.

readings.notes: On the grassroots right-to-information/social-audit movement that led to India's RTI Act; directly relevant to the governance area's transparency/accountability keywords.
readings.abstract: “Our money Our Accounts“ is a film made by Anurag Singh. Short film ( shorter version of the Right to Know ,Right to live) filmed at the time of the MKSS Campaign to access records from the Panchayat ( local self government ) presented  to the Standing Committee as part of its testimony by the NCPRI  in 2000- on peoples interface with transparency on public works and corruption. readings.title: What if the poor were part of city planning? | Smruti Jukur Johari
readings.notes: Intro-video framing content for the 2022 course's contemporary-challenges block.
readings.abstract:
Visit http://TED.com to get our entire library of TED Talks, transcripts, translations, personalized Talk recommendations and more.

Almost a billion people worldwide live in informal communities and slums, often without basic infrastructure like clean water, toilets or adequate roads. Urban planner Smruti Jukur Johari breaks down myths about these communities and shares examples of simple, common-sense solutions that arise when governments and architects work together with the residents -- instead of around them.

The TED Talks channel features the best talks and performances from the TED Conference, where the world's leading thinkers and doers give the talk of their lives in 18 minutes (or less). Look for talks on Technology, Entertainment and Design -- plus science, business, global issues, the arts and more. readings.title: A history of microfinance | Muhammad Yunus | TEDxVienna
readings.notes: Video on Muhammad Yunus's microfinance model, paired in the original course with the Mohd. Yunus / SKS Microfinance readings.
readings.abstract:
Prof. Muhammad Yunus is a Bangladeshi economist and founder of the Grameen Bank, an institution that provides microcredit to help its clients establish creditworthiness and financial self-sufficiency. In 2006 Yunus and Grameen received the Nobel Peace Prize. Yunus himself has received several other national and international honors.
He is a member of advisory board at Shahjalal University of Science and Technology. Previously, he was a professor of economics at Chittagong University where he developed the concepts of microcredit and microfinance. He is the author of Banker to the Poor and two books on Social Business Models, and a founding board member of Grameen America and Grameen Foundation.

This talk was given at a TEDx event using the TED conference format but independently organized by a local community. Learn more at http://ted.com/tedx

http://www.tedxvienna.at/

readings.notes: TED talk on the Barefoot College model (community-driven skill-building for rural/illiterate adults); paired with the livelihoods-sector student presentation block.

readings.title: OLPC Mission, Part 1: Principles and Child Empowerment
readings.notes: Paired with the education-sector student presentation block.
readings.abstract:
One Laptop per Child:  http://laptop.org

It's an education project, not a laptop project.  Inexpensive, durable,  networked laptops are important to better education everywhere in the world, empowering children and communities, and sharing access to modern skills with every child on the planet.

readings.title: OLPC Mission, Part 2: The XO Laptop, design for learning
readings.notes: Paired with the education-sector student presentation block; companion to '2015_one_laptop_per_child_demo_1'.
readings.abstract:
Why give a Laptop?  See http://laptop.org  and http://laptop.org/givemany

Substitute the word Laptop, with the word Education, and you have much of the answer.  XOs make it possible to collaborate, learn, teach, and publish at no cost.  They inspire new forms of learning, and attention to education.  And the provide access to digital texts in places too remote to send and update physical books in a cost-effective way.

readings.title: AutoTutor Demo 2002
readings.notes: Paired with the education-sector student presentation block.
readings.abstract: This is a demo from 2002. The conversation is an actual conversation with the tutor, although the student's voice has been added. Note the mixed initiative dialogue and question answering.

readings.title: AutoTutor Demo (10/16/2008)
readings.notes: Paired with the education-sector student presentation block; companion to '2015_intelligent_tutoring_demo_1'.
readings.abstract: An interactive session with an Intelligent Tutoring System with conversational dialog in natural language

readings.title: Technology crafts for the digitally underserved
readings.authors: Venkatraman, Vinay
readings.notes: Paired with the health-sector student presentation block.

readings.title: A warm embrace that saves lives
readings.authors: Chen, Jane
readings.notes: TED talk on the Embrace infant warmer, a frequently-cited frugal-innovation health device; paired with the health-sector student presentation block.

readings.title: The best stats you've ever seen
readings.authors: Rosling, Hans
readings.notes: Paired with the open-data student presentation block; general data-visualization-for-development framing.

readings.title: The next web
readings.authors: Berners-Lee, Tim
readings.notes: Paired with the open-data student presentation block.

readings.title: How open data is changing international aid
readings.authors: Pradhan, Sanjay
readings.notes: Paired with the open-data student presentation block.

readings.title: Revolutionlizing rural healthcare and beyond
readings.authors: Raghunath, B.B.
readings.notes: TEDx talk by a rural-healthcare physician on grassroots health delivery models in rural India; suggested addition for health-area problem discovery.
readings.abstract: A compassionate doctor, who has diligently worked towards the welfare of rural areas in several sectors, Dr. B. B. Raghunath believes that making the society inclusive is of utmost importance. Using an age old Indian tradition, he ushered in a mini revolution in the Health sector of Rural India.

readings.title: The most powerful untapped resource in health care
readings.authors: Elliott, Edith; Alam, Shahed
readings.notes: On Noora Health, which trains family caregivers of hospital patients in India and Bangladesh -- a strong ethnographic-design example (designing around existing family caregiving practice rather than assuming clinical staff capacity); suggested addition for health-area problem discovery / design.
readings.abstract: Whether we're rushing a child to the emergency room after a fall or making chicken soup for a feverish spouse, love inspires us to act when a family member gets sick. Global health activists Edith Elliott and Shahed Alam believe we can harness this power to create better health outcomes for everyone. Learn how their organization Noora Health works with doctors and nurses in India and Bangladesh to train the family members of hospital patients with essential skills to support their sick loved ones -- and how they plan to expand their reach to support 70 million caregivers who care for more than one billion people over the next six years.

readings.title: A Path to Social Safety for Migrant Workers | Ashif Shaikh | TED
readings.authors: Shaikh, Ashif
readings.notes: On the scale and precarity of India's internal (intra-country) migrant labour force. Year is a provisional estimate from video indexing metadata (YouTube link) -- run fetch_youtube_metadata.py to confirm exact upload date and get the full description.
readings.abstract:
Hundreds of millions of migrant workers travel within their countries to seek out means of survival — often leaving behind all they know for months or even years. Many face poverty and exploitation, and they need a robust social safety net to protect them, says migrant advocate and 2023 Audacious Project grantee Ashif Shaikh. He shares how his grassroots organization Migrants Resilience Collaborative is making life-changing benefits like social security and health care accessible to those who need them while also amplifying migrant voices — paving the way towards a world that supports the workers actually building it. (This ambitious idea is a part of the Audacious Project, TED's initiative to inspire and fund global change.)

readings.title: A bold plan to house 100 million people
readings.authors: Bhan, Gautam
readings.notes: TED Talks India (Star Plus), aired 11 Dec 2017. On the informal-settlement housing shortage facing India's cities and a policy vision for addressing it; directly relevant to the governance area's informal-settlements/urban-slum keywords.
readings.abstract: Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai, Kolkata -- all the major cities across India have one great thing in common: they welcome people arriving in search of work. But what lies at the other end of such openness and acceptance? Sadly, a shortage of housing for an estimated 100 million people, many of whom end up living in informal settlements. Gautam Bhan, a human settlement expert and researcher, is boldly reimagining a solution to this problem. He shares a new vision of urban India where everyone has a safe, sturdy home.

readings.title: The ancient ingenuity of water harvesting
readings.authors: Mishra, Anupam
readings.notes: Recorded at TED, Nov 2009 (published 3 Dec 2009). On centuries-old traditional rainwater harvesting engineering (stepwells, aqueducts) in the Thar/Golden Desert region of Rajasthan, often more effective and sustainable than modern water megaprojects -- an excellent traditional-knowledge counterpoint for the water_security area.
readings.abstract: With wisdom and wit, Anupam Mishra talks about the amazing feats of engineering built centuries ago by the people of India's Golden Desert to harvest water. These ancient aqueducts and stepwells are still used today -- and are often superior to modern water megaprojects.

readings.title: How we rescued the "dancing" bears
readings.authors: Satyanarayan, Kartick
readings.notes: Recorded at TEDIndia 2009, Mysore. On ending the centuries-old 'dancing bear' practice by making the Kalandar community (whose livelihood depended on it) part of the solution rather than treating them as the enemy -- a strong example of the livelihood/conservation tradeoff that recurs across natural-resource-management problems, and a natural pairing with Sekar's 'What's Left of the Jungle' already in the corpus.
readings.abstract: Traditionally, the Kalandar community of India has survived by capturing sloth bear cubs and training them to "dance" through extreme cruelty. Kartick Satyanarayan has been able to put an end to this centuries-old practice, and in so doing discovered a lesson of wider significance: make the practitioners part of the solution.

readings.title: A bold plan to empower 1.6 million out-of-school girls in India
readings.authors: Husain, Safeena
readings.notes: Part of TED's Audacious Project. Combines analytics-driven targeting with door-to-door community engagement to re-enroll out-of-school girls; directly addresses the education area's thinness and the gender axis together.
readings.abstract: "Girls' education is the closest thing we have to a silver bullet to help solve some of the world's most difficult problems," says social entrepreneur Safeena Husain. In a visionary talk, she shares her plan to enroll a staggering 1.6 million girls in school over the next five years -- combining advanced analytics with door-to-door community engagement to create new educational pathways for girls in India.

readings.title: How to fix a broken education system... without any more money
readings.authors: Bansal, Seema
readings.notes: On systemic public-school reform across 15,000 government schools in Haryana without additional funding -- relevant to both education-area problem discovery and operations/scaling of public service reform.
readings.abstract: Seema Bansal forged a path to public education reform for 15,000 schools in Haryana, India, by setting an ambitious goal: by 2020, 80 percent of children should have grade-level knowledge.

readings.title: MarginalizedAadhaar: How world's largest digital identification programme led to exclusions of Marginalised communities
readings.authors: Panigrahi, Subhashish
readings.notes: Open-access (CC-BY) documentary featuring women, Adivasi, transgender, and Dalit communities plus experts (incl. Usha Ramanathan) discussing Aadhaar exclusion. Directly resonates with the instructor's book, where Aadhaar is the recurring cautionary case study (Ch.4, Ch.6, Ch.7, Ch.11) for disempowerment through centralized, non-transparent, self-service system design that shifts power to intermediaries and excludes the rural poor.
readings.abstract: India's biometric-based ID -- Aadhaar -- has been widely discussed since its inception in 2008-2009. The documentary film evaluates the impact of India's biometric ID on some of the marginalised communities. Individuals representing historically marginalised groups such as women, Adivasi, transgender, Dalit, farmers, people with disability and different socio-economic groups, along with subject experts, discuss exclusion issues relevant to biometric ID implementation.

readings.title: Beware online "filter bubbles"
readings.authors: Pariser, Eli
readings.notes: The talk that coined 'filter bubble'. Directly resonates with the instructor's book's critique of Facebook (Ch.4: newsfeed design claims neutrality but does nothing to counter echo chambers/homophily; Ch.9: social media and democracy/polarization critique) -- a natural pairing with the existing Kumar (2014) Facebook and Adamic/Glance political-blogosphere readings already in the corpus.
readings.abstract: As web companies strive to tailor their services (including news and search results) to our personal tastes, there's a dangerous unintended consequence: we get trapped in a 'filter bubble' and don't get exposed to information that could challenge or broaden our worldview. Eli Pariser argues powerfully that this will ultimately prove to be bad for us and bad for democracy.

readings.title: COSMOS: Catching Out-of-Context Image Misuse Using Self-Supervised Learning
readings.authors: Shivangi Aneja, Chris Bregler, Matthias Niessner
readings.notes: The paper applies strong computer science methods (cs_fundamentals) that could be adapted to a developing-regions context to combat misinformation, which is crucial in low-resource settings with limited fact-checking resources.
readings.abstract: Abstract Despite the recent attention to DeepFakes, one of the most prevalent ways to mislead audiences on social media is the use of unaltered images in a new but false context. We propose a new method that automatically highlights out-of-context image and text pairs, for assisting fact-checkers. Our key insight is to leverage the grounding of images with text to distinguish out-of-context scenarios that cannot be disambiguated with language alone. We propose a self-supervised training strategy where we only need a set of captioned images. At train time, our method learns to selectively align individual objects in an image with textual claims, without explicit supervision. At test time, we check if both captions correspond to the same object(s) in the image but are semantically different, which allows us to make fairly accurate out-of-context predictions. Our method achieves 85% out-of-context detection accuracy. To facilitate benchmarking of this task, we create a large-scale dataset of 200K images with 450K textual captions from a variety of news websites, blogs, and social media posts

readings.title: Walkability Optimization: Formulations, Algorithms, and a Case Study of Toronto
readings.authors: Weimin Huang, Elias B. Khalil
readings.notes: The paper applies strong computer science methods (MILP, CP, greedy heuristic) to optimize walkability, which can be adapted to developing regions' urban planning and infrastructure challenges.
readings.abstract:
Abstract The concept of walkable urban development has gained increased
attention due to its public health, economic, and environmental
sustainability benefits. Unfortunately, land zoning
and historic under-investment have resulted in spatial inequality
in walkability and social inequality among residents.
We tackle the problem of Walkability Optimization through
the lens of combinatorial optimization. The task is to select
locations in which additional amenities (e.g., grocery stores,
schools, restaurants) can be allocated to improve resident access
via walking while taking into account existing amenities
and providing multiple options (e.g., for restaurants).
To this end, we derive Mixed-Integer Linear Programming
(MILP) and Constraint Programming (CP) models. Moreover,
we show that the problem’s objective function is submodular
in special cases, which motivates an efficient greedy
heuristic. We conduct a case study on 31 underserved neighborhoods
in the City of Toronto, Canada. MILP finds the
best solutions in most scenarios but does not scale well with
network size. The greedy algorithm scales well and finds
high-quality solutions. Our empirical evaluation shows that
neighbourhoods with low walkability have a great potential
for transformation into pedestrian-friendly neighbourhoods
by strategically placing new amenities. Allocating 3 additional
grocery stores, schools, and restaurants can improve the
“WalkScore” by more than 50 points (on a scale of 100) for 4
neighbourhoods and reduce the walking distances to amenities
for 75% of all residential locations to 10 minutes for all
amenity types. Our code and paper appendix are available at
https://github.com/khalil-research/walkability.

readings.title: Point-to-Region Co-learning for Poverty Mapping at High Resolution Using Satellite Imagery
readings.authors: Zhili Li, Yiqun Xie, Xiaowei Jia, Kara Stuart, Caroline Delaire, Sergii Skakun
readings.notes: The paper engages with a developing-regions context by aiming to generate fine-scale maps of vulnerable populations in low-income settlements in Africa, which is directly relevant to the 'water_security' and 'infrastructure' areas.
readings.abstract: Abstract Despite improvements in safe water and sanitation services in low-income countries, a substantial proportion of the population in Africa still does not have access to these essential services. Up-to-date fine-scale maps of low-income settlements are urgently needed by authorities  to improve service provision. We aim to develop a cost-effective solution to generate fine-scale maps of these vulnerable populations using multi-source public information. The problem is challenging as ground-truth maps are available at only a limited number of cities, and the patterns are heterogeneous across cities. Recent attempts tackling the spatial heterogeneity issue focus on scenarios where true labels partially exist for each input region, which are unavailable for the present problem. We propose a dynamic point-to-region co-learning framework to learn heterogeneity patterns that cannot be reflected by point-level information and generalize deep learners to new areas with no labels. We also propose an attention-based correction layer to remove spurious signatures, and a region-gate to capture both region-invariant and variant patterns. Experiment results on real-world fine-scale data in three cities of Kenya show that the proposed approach can largely improve model performance on various base network architectures.

readings.title: Bugs in the Data: How ImageNet Misrepresents Biodiversity
readings.authors: Alexandra Sasha Luccioni, David Rolnick
readings.notes: The paper engages with the use of machine learning datasets, which can be adapted to a developing-regions context for biodiversity and wildlife conservation tasks.
readings.abstract: Abstract ImageNet-1k is a dataset often used for benchmarking machine learning (ML) models and evaluating tasks such as image recognition and object detection. Wild animals make up 27% of ImageNet-1k but, unlike classes representing people and objects, these data have not been closely scrutinized. In the current paper, we analyze the 13,450 images from 269 classes that represent wild animals in the ImageNet-1k validation set, with the participation of expert ecologists. We find that many of the classes are ill-defined or overlapping, and that 12% of the images are incorrectly labeled, with some classes having >90% of images incorrect. We also find that both the wildlife-related labels and images included in ImageNet-1k present significant geographical and cultural biases, as well as ambiguities such as artificial animals, multiple species in the same image, or the presence of humans. Our findings highlight serious issues with the extensive use of this dataset for evaluating ML systems, the use of such algorithms in wildlife-related tasks, and more broadly the ways in which ML datasets are commonly created and curated.

readings.title: Joint Self-Supervised Image-Volume Representation Learning with Intra-inter Contrastive Clustering
readings.authors: Duy M. H. Nguyen, Hoang Nguyen, Truong T. N. Mai, Tri Cao, Binh T. Nguyen, Nhat Ho, Paul Swoboda, Shadi Albarqouni, Pengtao Xie, Daniel Sonntag
readings.notes: The paper applies strong computer science methods (cs_fundamentals) that could be adapted to a developing-regions context, as per the rule for this topic.
readings.abstract:
Abstract Collecting large-scale medical datasets with fully annotated samples for training of deep networks is prohibitively expensive, especially for 3D volume data. Recent breakthroughs in self-supervised learning (SSL) offer the ability to overcome the lack of labeled training samples by learning feature representations from unlabeled data. However, most current SSL techniques in the medical field have been designed for either 2D images or 3D volumes. In practice, this restricts the capability to fully leverage unlabeled data from numerous sources, which may include both 2D and 3D data. Additionally, the use of these pre-trained networks is constrained to downstream tasks with compatible data dimensions.
In this paper, we propose a novel framework for unsupervised joint learning on 2D and 3D data modalities. Given a set of 2D images or 2D slices extracted from 3D volumes, we construct an SSL task based on a 2D contrastive clustering problem for distinct classes. The 3D volumes are exploited by computing vectored embedding at each slice and then assembling a holistic feature through deformable self-attention mechanisms in Transformer, allowing incorporating long-range dependencies between slices inside 3D volumes. These holistic features are further utilized to define a novel 3D clustering agreement-based SSL task and masking embedding prediction inspired by pre-trained language models. Experiments on downstream tasks, such as 3D brain segmentation, lung nodule detection, 3D heart structures segmentation, and abnormal chest X-ray detection, demonstrate the effectiveness of our joint 2D and 3D SSL approach. We improve plain 2D Deep-ClusterV2 and SwAV by a significant margin and also surpass various modern 2D and 3D SSL approaches.

readings.title: Weather2vec: Representation Learning for Causal Inference with Non-local Confounding in Air Pollution and Climate Studies
readings.authors: Mauricio Tec, James G. Scott, Corwin M. Zigler
readings.notes: The paper provides a general-purpose methodology that could be adapted to a developing-regions context for causal inference in environmental and climate studies, aligning with the cs_fundamentals topic rule.
readings.abstract: Abstract Estimating the causal effects of a spatially-varying intervention on a spatially-varying outcome may be subject to non-local confounding (NLC), a phenomenon that can bias estimates when the treatments and outcomes of a given unit are dictated in part by the covariates of other nearby units. In particular, NLC is a challenge for evaluating the effects of environmental policies and climate events on health-related outcomes such as air pollution exposure. This paper first formalizes NLC using the potential outcomes framework, providing a comparison with the related phenomenon of causal interference. Then, it proposes a broadly applicable framework, termed weather2vec, that uses the theory of balancing scores to learn representations of non-local information into a scalar or vector defined for each observational unit, which is subsequently used to adjust for confounding in conjunction with causal inference methods. The framework is evaluated in a simulation study and two case studies on air pollution where the weather is an (inherently regional) known confounder.

readings.title: Evaluating Digital Agriculture Recommendations with Causal Inference
readings.authors: Ilias Tsoumas, Georgios Giannarakis, Vasileios Sitokonstantinou, Alkiviadis Koukos, Dimitra Loka, Nikolaos Bartsotas, Charalampos Kontoes, Ioannis Athanasiadis
readings.notes: The paper applies causal inference methods to evaluate the impact of digital agriculture tools, which is relevant to the 'cs_fundamentals' topic as it uses strong computer science methods to solve a problem in agriculture.
readings.abstract: Abstract In contrast to the rapid digitalization of several industries, agriculture suffers from low adoption of smart farming tools. Even though recent advancements in AI-driven digital agriculture can offer high-performing predictive functionalities, they lack tangible quantitative evidence on their benefits to the farmers. Field experiments can derive such evidence, but are often costly, time consuming and hence limited in scope and scale of application. To this end, we propose an observational causal inference framework for the empirical evaluation of the impact of digital tools on target farm performance indicators (e.g., yield in this case). This way, we can increase farmers' trust via enhancing the transparency of the digital agriculture market, and in turn accelerate the adoption of technologies that aim to secure farmer income resilience and global agricultural sustainability against a changing climate. As a case study, we designed and implemented a recommendation system for the optimal sowing time of cotton based on numerical weather predictions, which was used by a farmers' cooperative during the growing season of 2021. We then leverage agricultural knowledge, collected yield data, and environmental information to develop a causal graph of the farm system. Using the back-door criterion, we identify the impact of sowing recommendations on the yield and subsequently estimate it using linear regression, matching, inverse propensity score weighting and meta-learners. The results revealed that a field sown according to our recommendations exhibited a statistically significant yield increase that ranged from 12% to 17%, depending on the method. The effect estimates were robust, as indicated by the agreement among the estimation methods and four successful refutation tests. We argue that this approach can be implemented for decision support systems of other fields, extending their evaluation beyond a performance assessment of internal functionalities.

readings.title: Auto-CM: Unsupervised Deep Learning for Satellite Imagery Composition and Cloud Masking Using Spatio-Temporal Dynamics
readings.authors: Yiqun Xie, Zhili Li, Han Bao, Xiaowei Jia, Dongkuan Xu, Xun Zhou, Sergii Skakun
readings.notes: The paper applies unsupervised deep learning methods to satellite imagery, which can be adapted to a developing-regions context for tasks like agriculture monitoring and water security.
readings.abstract: Abstract Cloud masking is both a fundamental and a critical task in the vast majority of Earth observation problems across social sectors, including agriculture, energy, water, etc. The sheer volume of satellite imagery to be processed has fast-climbed to a scale (e.g., >10 PBs/year) that is prohibitive for manual processing. Meanwhile, generating reliable cloud masks and image composite is increasingly challenging due to the continued distribution-shifts in the imagery collected by existing sensors and the ever-growing variety of sensors and platforms. Moreover, labeled samples are scarce and geographically limited compared to the needs in real large-scale applications. In related work, traditional remote sensing methods are often physics-based and rely on special spectral signatures from multi- or hyper-spectral bands, which are often not available in data collected by many -- and especially more recent -- high-resolution platforms. Machine learning and deep learning based methods, on the other hand, often require large volumes of up-to-date training data to be reliable and generalizable over space. We propose an autonomous image composition and masking (Auto-CM) framework to learn to solve the fundamental tasks in a label-free manner, by leveraging different dynamics of events in both geographic domains and time-series. Our experiments show that Auto-CM outperforms existing methods on a wide-range of data with different satellite platforms, geographic regions and bands.

readings.title: Deep Learning on a Healthy Data Diet: Finding Important Examples for Fairness
readings.authors: Abdelrahman Zayed, Prasanna Parthasarathi, Gonçalo Mordido, Hamid Palangi, Samira Shabanian, Sarath Chandar
readings.notes: The paper addresses gender bias in NLP, which is relevant to the 'gender' cross-cutting axis in the context of ICT for Development and Sustainability.
readings.abstract: Abstract Data-driven predictive solutions predominant in commercial applications tend to suffer from biases and stereotypes, which raises equity concerns. Prediction models may discover, use, or amplify spurious correlations based on gender or other protected personal characteristics, thus discriminating against marginalized groups. Mitigating gender bias has become an important research focus in natural language processing (NLP) and is an area where annotated corpora are available. Data augmentation reduces gender bias by adding counterfactual examples to the training dataset. In this work, we show that some of the examples in the augmented dataset can be not important or even harmful to fairness. We hence propose a general method for pruning both the factual and counterfactual examples to maximize the model’s fairness as measured by the demographic parity, equality of opportunity, and equality of odds. The fairness achieved by our method surpasses that of data augmentation on three text classification datasets, using no more than half of the examples in the augmented dataset. Our experiments are conducted using models of varying sizes and pre-training settings. WARNING: This work uses language that is offensive in nature.

readings.title: A Continual Pre-training Approach to Tele-Triaging Pregnant Women in Kenya
readings.authors: Wenbo Zhang, Hangzhi Guo, Prerna Ranganathan, Jay Patel, Sathyanath Rajasekharan, Nidhi Danayak, Manan Gupta, Amulya Yadav
readings.notes: The paper engages with a developing-regions context (Kenya) and applies strong computer science methods to solve a health-related problem, fitting the 'cs_fundamentals' topic rule.
readings.abstract: Abstract Access to high-quality maternal health care services is limited in Kenya, which resulted in ∼36,000 maternal and neonatal deaths in 2018. To tackle this challenge, Jacaranda Health (a non-profit organization working on maternal health in Kenya) developed PROMPTS, an SMS based tele-triage system for pregnant and puerperal women, which has more than 350,000 active users in Kenya. PROMPTS empowers pregnant women living far away from doctors and hospitals to send SMS messages to get quick answers (through human helpdesk agents) to questions about their medical symptoms and pregnancy status. Unfortunately, ∼1.1 million SMS messages are received by PROMPTS every month, which makes it challenging for helpdesk agents to ensure that these messages can be interpreted correctly and evaluated by their level of emergency to ensure timely responses and/or treatments for women in need.
